questions.prompt: Are you asking about your own record?
options: I am helping someone complete their own check

questions.prompt: Did this case happen in New Mexico (not a federal case)?

options: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar program
Juvenile adjudication or offense committed as a minor

Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or.label: The case was dropped or thrown out
Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or.helperText: dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted

Diversion, deferred disposition, supervi.label: Completed a program instead of a conviction
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervi.helperText: diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar

Juvenile adjudication or offense committ.label: It happened when I was a minor
Juvenile adjudication or offense committ.helperText: juvenile adjudication or offense as a minor

I am not sure.label: I'm not sure
I am not sure.helperText: That's okay — we'll help you figure it out

questions.prompt: What kind of charge was it?
Do any of these sound like your situation?
options: None of these / I am not sure
questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: Step 1 - Was the person wrongly identified because of identity theft?
How old were you when this happened?
Have you finished everything the court ordered?
questions.helperText: jail/probation/parole, classes, community service, anything still owed

questions.prompt: Have you paid off everything the court charged?
Do you have any open cases right now?
questions.helperText: a charge in progress, or current probation/parole

questions.prompt: Was this arrest a mistake — wrong person, identity theft, or an error?
When did the case end or finish?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What does the record say you were charged with?
questions.helperText: the charge name or code, however it's written

questions.prompt: Do you have your court paperwork handy?
questions.helperText: No worries if not — you can save and come back.

questions.prompt: Where in New Mexico did the case happen?

questions.helperText: also called a docket, cause, or arrest number — it's on your court paperwork

caseOutcomeOptions.label: Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar program
Juvenile adjudication or offense committed as a minor

pathways.label: No conviction / released without conviction
pathways.summary: A person released without conviction may file one year after the final disposition in the district where the charges originated. This route covers municipal ordinance violations, misdemeanors, felonies, certain Criminal Code or Motor Vehicle Code penalty assessments, and Motor Vehicle Code violations/deferred sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")
ruleClauses: A person released without conviction may file one year after the final disposition in the district where the charges originated. This route covers municipal ordinance violations, misdemeanors, felonies, certain Criminal Code or Motor Vehicle Code penalty assessments, and Motor Vehicle Code violations/deferred sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")

includes acquittal or finding of not guilty, nolle prosequi, no bill or other dismissal, referral to a preprosecution diversion program, conditional discharge under 31-20-13, or proceedings otherwise discharged. If the court finds no other charge or proceeding is pending and the person was released without conviction, the court must issue an order within 30 days of the hearing requiring expungement of related arrest and public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")
Waiting period: 1 year from final disposition.
Required notice: district attorney and Department of Public Safety.
Required record attachment: DPS record of arrest and prosecutions dated no earlier than 90 days before filing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")

If your New Mexico case was dismissed, you were found not guilty, the case was no-billed, you completed certain diversion or conditional-discharge routes, or the case was otherwise discharged, you may be able to file after one year from the final disposition, as long as you have no pending charges or proceedings.
waitingRules: A person released without conviction may file one year after the final disposition in the district where the charges originated. This route covers municipal ordinance violations, misdemeanors, felonies, certain Criminal Code or Motor Vehicle Code penalty assessments, and Motor Vehicle Code violations/deferred sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")

includes acquittal or finding of not guilty, nolle prosequi, no bill or other dismissal, referral to a preprosecution diversion program, conditional discharge under 31-20-13, or proceedings otherwise discharged. If the court finds no other charge or proceeding is pending and the person was released without conviction, the court must issue an order within 30 days of the hearing requiring expungement of related arrest and public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")
Waiting period: 1 year from final disposition.
Required record attachment: DPS record of arrest and prosecutions dated no earlier than 90 days before filing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")

If your New Mexico case was dismissed, you were found not guilty, the case was no-billed, you completed certain diversion or conditional-discharge routes, or the case was otherwise discharged, you may be able to file after one year from the final disposition, as long as you have no pending charges or proceedings.

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

pathways.label: Conviction
pathways.summary: A person convicted of a municipal ordinance violation, misdemeanor, or felony may petition after completing the sentence and paying fines or fees owed to the state for that conviction. The petition is filed in the district court where the person was convicted, and notice goes to the district attorney, DPS, and the law enforcement agency that arrested the person. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/") The court must find: no other charge or proceeding…
ruleClauses: A person convicted of a municipal ordinance violation, misdemeanor, or felony may petition after completing the sentence and paying fines or fees owed to the state for that conviction. The petition is filed in the district court where the person was convicted, and notice goes to the district attorney, DPS, and the law enforcement agency that arrested the person. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")

The court must find: no other charge or proceeding is pending; justice will be served; victim restitution has been fulfilled; and no other criminal conviction occurred during the applicable waiting period. The statute measures the waiting period from the last date on which the person completed a sentence for a conviction in any jurisdiction. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
Fourth-degree felony not otherwise listed 4 years
Third-degree felony not otherwise listed 6 years
Second-degree felony not otherwise listed 8 years
Crimes Against Household Members Act offense 10 years

For New Mexico conviction expungement, the clock usually starts after the sentence is completed, not just from the conviction date. I need the conviction level, sentence-completion date, whether fines and fees are paid, whether restitution is complete, and whether you have had any new convictions during the waiting period.
waitingRules: The court must find: no other charge or proceeding is pending; justice will be served; victim restitution has been fulfilled; and no other criminal conviction occurred during the applicable waiting period. The statute measures the waiting period from the last date on which the person completed a sentence for a conviction in any jurisdiction. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
Fourth-degree felony not otherwise listed 4 years
Third-degree felony not otherwise listed 6 years
Second-degree felony not otherwise listed 8 years
Crimes Against Household Members Act offense 10 years

pathways.label: Cannabis expungement
pathways.summary: New Mexico has a special cannabis pathway. If a person was charged with an offense involving cannabis that is no longer a crime as of June 29, 2021, or would have resulted in a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, public records relating to the arrest or conviction are automatically expunged two years after the conviction date, or two years after the arrest date if there was no conviction. For minors, the record is retained for two years or until the person turns 18, whichever…
ruleClauses: New Mexico has a special cannabis pathway. If a person was charged with an offense involving cannabis that is no longer a crime as of June 29, 2021, or would have resulted in a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, public records relating to the arrest or conviction are automatically expunged two years after the conviction date, or two years after the arrest date if there was no conviction. For minors, the record is retained for two years or until the person turns 18, whichever comes first, and then automatically expunged. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-8/")

Automatic expungement under 29-3A-8 applies to public records involving only cannabis and cannabis paraphernalia charges and requires destruction of those records. The Administrative Office of the Courts must provide a procedure for eligible people to verify whether automatic expungement occurred and request expedited automatic expungement if it has not. If the arrest or conviction involved both cannabis and non-cannabis charges, the person may request expungement of the eligible cannabis charges through the AOC process. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-8/")

New Mexico has a special cannabis expungement process. If the case involved only cannabis or cannabis paraphernalia charges that are no longer crimes or would now be lesser offenses, it may be eligible for automatic or expedited automatic expungement. If the case also had non-cannabis charges, we may need to separate the eligible cannabis charges from the rest of the case.
waitingRules: New Mexico has a special cannabis pathway. If a person was charged with an offense involving cannabis that is no longer a crime as of June 29, 2021, or would have resulted in a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, public records relating to the arrest or conviction are automatically expunged two years after the conviction date, or two years after the arrest date if there was no conviction. For minors, the record is retained for two years or until the person turns 18, whichever comes first, and then automatically expunged. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-8/")

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

pathways.label: Cannabis sentence dismissal / incarcerated-person pathway
pathways.summary: For people currently incarcerated for a cannabis offense that is no longer a crime or would have been a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, correctional facilities, jails, or juvenile correctional facilities were required to notify the court that the case may be reopened for dismissal of sentence or expungement. The court must reopen and dismiss the sentence if it is legally invalid. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-9/") A person who…
ruleClauses: For people currently incarcerated for a cannabis offense that is no longer a crime or would have been a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, correctional facilities, jails, or juvenile correctional facilities were required to notify the court that the case may be reopened for dismissal of sentence or expungement. The court must reopen and dismiss the sentence if it is legally invalid. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-9/")

A person who completed a sentence for a cannabis conviction that would no longer be an offense or would be a lesser offense is entitled to have the conviction dismissed and expunged because the prior conviction is legally invalid or redesignated as a penalty assessment citation. A person currently or formerly incarcerated for a cannabis offense that is no longer a crime may petition at any time to modify the sentence or vacate the conviction, and no fee or cost may be imposed for sentence review under this section. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-9/")

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

pathways.label: DNA sample/profile expungement
pathways.summary: DNA sample/profile expungement is separate from the ordinary court-record expungement. A person may request expungement of their DNA sample and DNA records from New Mexicos DNA identification system if the conviction that led to the sample was reversed, or if the arrest that led to the sample resulted in dismissal, nolle prosequi, successful pre-prosecution diversion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or did not result in a felony charge within one year.…
ruleClauses: DNA sample/profile expungement is separate from the ordinary court-record expungement. A person may request expungement of their DNA sample and DNA records from New Mexicos DNA identification system if the conviction that led to the sample was reversed, or if the arrest that led to the sample resulted in dismissal, nolle prosequi, successful pre-prosecution diversion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or did not result in a felony charge within one year. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")

The person must provide a written request and certified documentation, such as a certified copy of the dismissal/nolle prosequi/diversion/conditional discharge/misdemeanor conviction/acquittal, or a sworn affidavit that no felony charges were filed within one year. If DNA records are expunged from the state DNA identification system, the administrative center must ensure expungement from CODIS. DNA expungement is not available if the person has a prior felony conviction or pending felony charge for which DNA collection is authorized. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")
waitingRules: DNA sample/profile expungement is separate from the ordinary court-record expungement. A person may request expungement of their DNA sample and DNA records from New Mexicos DNA identification system if the conviction that led to the sample was reversed, or if the arrest that led to the sample resulted in dismissal, nolle prosequi, successful pre-prosecution diversion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or did not result in a felony charge within one year. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")

The person must provide a written request and certified documentation, such as a certified copy of the dismissal/nolle prosequi/diversion/conditional discharge/misdemeanor conviction/acquittal, or a sworn affidavit that no felony charges were filed within one year. If DNA records are expunged from the state DNA identification system, the administrative center must ensure expungement from CODIS. DNA expungement is not available if the person has a prior felony conviction or pending felony charge for which DNA collection is authorized. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")

when.sourceConditionText: Conviction expungement 29-3A-5 Eligible municipal ordinance, misdemeanor, or felony convictions after waiting period
Cannabis automatic / administrative expungement 29-3A-8 Cannabis/cannabis paraphernalia charges no longer criminal or now lesser offense

New Mexico uses the word expungement, but for most non-cannabis criminal records it means removing records from general public access, not physically destroying every record. The statute defines expungement as removal from public access of notations of arrest, complaint, indictment, information, guilty plea, conviction, acquittal, dismissal, or discharge, including records posted on public court, corrections, or law-enforcement websites. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-2/")

In New Mexico, expungement usually means the general public should no longer be able to access the arrest or court record. It does not always mean the record is destroyed. Courts, law enforcement, and other criminal justice agencies may still have access in some situations.

New Mexicos court guidance says the district court cannot expunge records from another state or federal records. It also says the petition must be filed in the district where the arrest and/or criminal records originated, even if the original case was in municipal or metropolitan court.
Release without conviction 29-3A-4 Dismissal, acquittal, nolle prosequi, no bill, diversion, conditional discharge, or other discharge
Cannabis sentence dismissal / vacatur for incarcerated or formerly incarcerated people 29-3A-9 Cannabis conviction/sentence now legally invalid or reducible after Cannabis Regulation Act
DNA sample/profile expungement 29-16-10 Separate request to remove DNA sample/profile from DNA identification system/CODIS

A person wrongly identified in arrest records or public records because of identity theft may petition the district court for expungement. After a hearing and a showing that the person is a victim of identity theft, the court must issue an order within 30 days requiring all arrest records and public records to be expunged. The court must also cause the order to be delivered to relevant law enforcement agencies and courts. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-3/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_identity_theft_expungement

If someone used your identity and the record wrongly names you, New Mexico has a specific identity-theft expungement route. We need proof that you were the victim of identity theft and records showing which arrest or court records identify you incorrectly.
Step 2 - Was there a conviction?
1 Form 4-951 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Identity Theft
1 Form 4-952 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Upon Release Without Conviction
1 Form 4-953 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Upon Conviction

Use the New Mexico Courts cannabis expungement application/AOC process first when the case is cannabis-only or cannabis-paraphernalia-only. If the case includes non-cannabis charges, route to the AOC cannabis request for eligible cannabis charges and separately screen non-cannabis charges under the regular court-petition process. ("https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/")
1 Written request for DNA sample/profile expungement.
2 Certified copy of dismissal, nolle prosequi, diversion completion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or reversal order.
3 Sworn affidavit if no felony charge was filed within one year.
4 Screen for prior felony conviction or pending felony charge, which can block DNA expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")
No conviction / released without conviction
Conviction
Cannabis expungement
Cannabis sentence dismissal / incarcerated-person pathway
DNA sample/profile expungement
No source-defined jurisdiction pathway matched the submitted facts.

exclusionRules.ruleText: New Mexicos court guidance says the district court cannot expunge records from another state or federal records. It also says the petition must be filed in the district where the arrest and/or criminal records originated, even if the original case was in municipal or metropolitan court.

Filing location and packet basics A New Mexico expungement petition is filed in district court in the district where the arrest and/or criminal records originated. This is true even if the original case was in municipal court, magistrate court, metropolitan court, or was dismissed before the DA or city attorney filed a court case. New Mexico court guidance says multiple charges and arrests under the same case number may be included in one petition, but if the person seeks to expunge arrest records not associated with a filed case, they must file a separate expungement petition for each arrest. The petition and information provided during the process are sealed from public access even if the petition is denied. The current general court information sheet lists a $132 district court filing fee and says a person who cannot pay should complete an Application for Free Process and Affidavit of Indigency, Form 4-222 NMRA. It also says a DPS arrest-record check costs $15 and requires an Authorization for Release of Information form with an original notarized signature.
New Mexico conviction expungement does not apply to convictions for:

New Mexico has several expungement routes. If your case ended without a conviction, you may be able to file one year after the final disposition if you have no pending charges. If you were convicted, the waiting period depends on the level of the offense - usually two, four, six, eight, or ten years after sentence completion - and the court must also find that justice will be served. Some convictions, including DWI/DUI, sex offenses, offenses against children, embezzlement, and offenses causing great bodily harm or death, are not eligible. Cannabis records have a separate automatic or administrative process, and DNA records have a separate request process. Ill need your case number, county, charge, final disposition, sentence-completion date, and DPS record to screen the correct path.
New Mexicos court guidance says the district court cannot expunge records from another state or federal records.

The current general court information sheet lists a $132 district court filing fee and says a person who cannot pay should complete an Application for Free Process and Affidavit of Indigency, Form 4-222 NMRA.
Conviction exclusions New Mexico conviction expungement does not apply to convictions for: An offense committed against a child.

An offense involving driving while under the influence of intoxicating liquor or drugs. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/") Wilma hard-stop / legal-review labels: excluded_offense_against_child excluded_great_bodily_harm_or_death excluded_sex_offense excluded_embezzlement excluded_dwi_dui legal_review_required_unclear_exclusion Wilma script: New Mexico excludes some convictions from expungement, including DWI/DUI, embezzlement, sex offenses, offenses against children, and offenses that caused great bodily harm or death.

sourceFormStatements: New Mexico Courts currently lists these petition forms for non-cannabis court petition expungements:
4-951 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; identity theft 29-3A-3 identity-theft route
4-952 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon release without conviction 29-3A-4 no-conviction route
4-953 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon conviction 29-3A-5 conviction route

New Mexico Courts expungement page lists those three petitions and says the court-petition expungement process requires filing at a courthouse. The same page says the automatic case-record expungement process is only for cannabis/marijuana, and if the court record does not specifically state cannabis or cannabis paraphernalia, the person may still request expungement using the regular court petition process. (

For cannabis records, New Mexico Courts provides an Application for Expungement of Court Records involving Cannabis and asks for the users name, date of birth, full case number, last four digits of SSN, address, phone/email, picture ID, and case documentation if available. The court page also says users can use Case Lookup to find the case number. ("https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/")

New Mexico Courts currently lists these petition forms for non-cannabis court petition expungements: Form Official name Use 4-951 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; identity theft 29-3A-3 identity-theft route 4-952 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon release without conviction 29-3A-4 no-conviction route 4-953 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon conviction 29-3A-5 conviction route New Mexico Courts expungement page lists those three petitions and says the court-petition expungement process requires filing at a courthouse.

The same page says the automatic case-record expungement process is only for cannabis/marijuana, and if the court record does not specifically state cannabis or cannabis paraphernalia, the person may still request expungement using the regular court petition process. ( "https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/") For cannabis records, New Mexico Courts provides an Application for Expungement of Court Records involving Cannabis and asks for the users name, date of birth, full case number, last four digits of SSN, address, phone/email, picture ID, and case documentation if available.
attachments: 4 Certificate/service to district attorney and DPS.
2 Certified copy of dismissal, nolle prosequi, diversion completion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or reversal order.
Conviction Generate/check: 1 Form 4-953 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Upon Conviction 2 DPS record / criminal history.
hearingAndObjectionRules: 6 Notice of hearing or local hearing documents.
postFilingRules: After an expungement order, proceedings are treated as if they never occurred, and officials and the person who received the order may respond to an inquiry that no record exists. However, arrest or conviction records must still be disclosed by the person and officials for applications or queries involving employment or association with a financial institution regulated by FINRA or the Securities and Exchange Commission. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-7/")

New Mexico court guidance also warns that courts, law enforcement agencies, and other criminal justice agencies will always have access to records even after expungement; records are not destroyed; and arrest or conviction records may still be available for use in future criminal proceedings.

Expungement can help remove the record from public view and allow most public-facing responses to say no record exists. But it is not absolute. Criminal justice agencies may still access it, it may be used in future criminal proceedings, and financial-industry applications may still require disclosure.
After an expungement order, proceedings are treated as if they never occurred, and officials and the person who received the order may respond to an inquiry that no record exists.

However, arrest or conviction records must still be disclosed by the person and officials for applications or queries involving employment or association with a financial institution regulated by FINRA or the Securities and Exchange Commission. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-7/") New Mexico court guidance also warns that courts, law enforcement agencies, and other criminal justice agencies will always have access to records even after expungement; records are not destroyed; and arrest or conviction records may still be available for use in future criminal proceedings.
Wilma plain-language script: Expungement can help remove the record from public view and allow most public-facing responses to say no record exists.

copyGuardrails: It does not always mean the record is destroyed.
After an expungement order, proceedings are treated as if they never occurred, and officials and the person who received the order may respond to an inquiry that no record exists.

However, arrest or conviction records must still be disclosed by the person and officials for applications or queries involving employment or association with a financial institution regulated by FINRA or the Securities and Exchange Commission. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-7/") New Mexico court guidance also warns that courts, law enforcement agencies, and other criminal justice agencies will always have access to records even after expungement; records are not destroyed; and arrest or conviction records may still be available for use in future criminal proceedings.
Use New Mexico-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: Core New Mexico rule
sourceSections.text:
New Mexico uses the word expungement, but for most non-cannabis criminal records it means removing records from general public access, not physically destroying every record. The statute defines expungement as removal from public access of notations of arrest, complaint, indictment, information, guilty plea, conviction, acquittal, dismissal, or discharge, including records posted on public court, corrections, or law-enforcement websites. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-2/")
Wilma plain-language script:
In New Mexico, expungement usually means the general public should no longer be able to access the arrest or court record. It does not always mean the record is destroyed. Courts, law enforcement, and other criminal justice agencies may still have access in some situations.

New Mexicos court guidance says the district court cannot expunge records from another state or federal records. It also says the petition must be filed in the district where the arrest and/or criminal records originated, even if the original case was in municipal or metropolitan court.

sourceSections.title: Main New Mexico pathways
sourceSections.text:
Pathway Main authority Best used for
Identity-theft / wrong-person records 29-3A-3 Someone was wrongly identified because of identity theft
Release without conviction 29-3A-4 Dismissal, acquittal, nolle prosequi, no bill, diversion, conditional discharge, or other discharge
Conviction expungement 29-3A-5 Eligible municipal ordinance, misdemeanor, or felony convictions after waiting period
Cannabis automatic / administrative expungement 29-3A-8 Cannabis/cannabis paraphernalia charges no longer criminal or now lesser offense
Cannabis sentence dismissal / vacatur for incarcerated or formerly incarcerated people 29-3A-9 Cannabis conviction/sentence now legally invalid or reducible after Cannabis Regulation Act
DNA sample/profile expungement 29-16-10 Separate request to remove DNA sample/profile from DNA identification system/CODIS

sourceSections.title: Official forms Wilma should know
sourceSections.text:
New Mexico Courts currently lists these petition forms for non-cannabis court petition expungements:
Form Official name Use
4-951 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; identity theft 29-3A-3 identity-theft route
4-952 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon release without conviction 29-3A-4 no-conviction route
4-953 Petition to expunge arrest records and public records; upon conviction 29-3A-5 conviction route
New Mexico Courts expungement page lists those three petitions and says the court-petition expungement process requires filing at a courthouse. The same page says the automatic case-record expungement process is only for cannabis/marijuana, and if the court record does not specifically state cannabis or cannabis paraphernalia, the person may still request expungement using the regular court petition process. (

"https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/")
For cannabis records, New Mexico Courts provides an Application for Expungement of Court Records involving Cannabis and asks for the users name, date of birth, full case number, last four digits of SSN, address, phone/email, picture ID, and case documentation if available. The court page also says users can use Case Lookup to find the case number. ("https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/")

sourceSections.title: Decision tree for Wilma
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Was the person wrongly identified because of identity theft?
A person wrongly identified in arrest records or public records because of identity theft may petition the district court for expungement. After a hearing and a showing that the person is a victim of identity theft, the court must issue an order within 30 days requiring all arrest records and public records to be expunged. The court must also cause the order to be delivered to relevant law enforcement agencies and courts. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-3/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_identity_theft_expungement
Wilma script:
If someone used your identity and the record wrongly names you, New Mexico has a specific identity-theft expungement route. We need proof that you were the victim of identity theft and records showing which arrest or court records identify you incorrectly.

Step 2 - Was there a conviction?

sourceSections.title: No conviction / released without conviction
sourceSections.text:
A person released without conviction may file one year after the final disposition in the district where the charges originated. This route covers municipal ordinance violations, misdemeanors, felonies, certain Criminal Code or Motor Vehicle Code penalty assessments, and Motor Vehicle Code violations/deferred sentences. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")

Released without conviction
includes acquittal or finding of not guilty, nolle prosequi, no bill or other dismissal, referral to a preprosecution diversion program, conditional discharge under 31-20-13, or proceedings otherwise discharged. If the court finds no other charge or proceeding is pending and the person was released without conviction, the court must issue an order within 30 days of the hearing requiring expungement of related arrest and public records. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")
Waiting period: 1 year from final disposition.
Required notice: district attorney and Department of Public Safety.
Required record attachment: DPS record of arrest and prosecutions dated no earlier than 90 days before filing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-4/")
Wilma status labels:
likely_eligible_release_without_conviction
not_eligible_yet_one_year_from_final_disposition
not_eligible_pending_charge_or_proceeding
needs_final_disposition
needs_dps_record_within_90_days
Wilma script:
If your New Mexico case was dismissed, you were found not guilty, the case was no-billed, you completed certain diversion or conditional-discharge routes, or the case was otherwise discharged, you may be able to file after one year from the final disposition, as long as you have no pending charges or proceedings.

sourceSections.title: Conviction
sourceSections.text:
A person convicted of a municipal ordinance violation, misdemeanor, or felony may petition after completing the sentence and paying fines or fees owed to the state for that conviction. The petition is filed in the district court where the person was convicted, and notice goes to the district attorney, DPS, and the law enforcement agency that arrested the person. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
The court must find: no other charge or proceeding is pending; justice will be served; victim restitution has been fulfilled; and no other criminal conviction occurred during the applicable waiting period. The statute measures the waiting period from the last date on which the person completed a sentence for a conviction in any jurisdiction. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
Conviction waiting periods

Conviction type Waiting period
Municipal ordinance conviction 2 years
Misdemeanor not otherwise listed 2 years
Misdemeanor aggravated battery under 30-3-5(B) 4 years
Fourth-degree felony not otherwise listed 4 years
Third-degree felony not otherwise listed 6 years
Second-degree felony not otherwise listed 8 years
First-degree felony 10 years
Crimes Against Household Members Act offense 10 years
These waiting periods come from 29-3A-5(C)(4). (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
Wilma status labels:
likely_eligible_conviction_2_year_misdemeanor_or_municipal
likely_eligible_conviction_4_year_aggravated_battery_or_4th_degree_felony
likely_eligible_conviction_6_year_3rd_degree_felony
likely_eligible_conviction_8_year_2nd_degree_felony
likely_eligible_conviction_10_year_1st_degree_or_household_member_act
not_eligible_yet_waiting_period
not_eligible_pending_charge_or_proceeding
not_eligible_unpaid_fines_fees_or_restitution
needs_sentence_completion_date
needs_restitution_status
Wilma script:
For New Mexico conviction expungement, the clock usually starts after the sentence is completed, not just from the conviction date. I need the conviction level, sentence-completion date, whether fines and fees are paid, whether restitution is complete, and whether you have had any new convictions during the waiting period.

sourceSections.title: Conviction exclusions
sourceSections.text:
New Mexico conviction expungement does not apply to convictions for:
An offense committed against a child.
An offense that caused great bodily harm or death to another person.
A sex offense as defined in 29-11A-3.
Embezzlement under 30-16-8.
An offense involving driving while under the influence of intoxicating liquor or drugs. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-5/")
Wilma hard-stop / legal-review labels:
excluded_offense_against_child
excluded_great_bodily_harm_or_death
excluded_sex_offense
excluded_embezzlement
excluded_dwi_dui
legal_review_required_unclear_exclusion
Wilma script:
New Mexico excludes some convictions from expungement, including DWI/DUI, embezzlement, sex offenses, offenses against children, and offenses that caused great bodily harm or death. If any of those may apply, this needs legal review before we promise any pathway.

Important DWI nuance: The definition of
arrest records
excludes DWI citations maintained by the Taxation and Revenue Department, computer-aided dispatch information, and breath alcohol testing equipment log books. So even when a non-conviction case has an expungement path, some DWI-related records may remain outside the standard expungement definition. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-2/")

sourceSections.title: Cannabis expungement
sourceSections.text:
New Mexico has a special cannabis pathway. If a person was charged with an offense involving cannabis that is no longer a crime as of June 29, 2021, or would have resulted in a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, public records relating to the arrest or conviction are automatically expunged two years after the conviction date, or two years after the arrest date if there was no conviction. For minors, the record is retained for two years or until the person turns 18, whichever comes first, and then automatically expunged. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-8/")
Automatic expungement under 29-3A-8 applies to public records involving only cannabis and cannabis paraphernalia charges and requires destruction of those records. The Administrative Office of the Courts must provide a procedure for eligible people to verify whether automatic expungement occurred and request expedited automatic expungement if it has not. If the arrest or conviction involved both cannabis and non-cannabis charges, the person may request expungement of the eligible cannabis charges through the AOC process. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-8/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_automatic_cannabis_expungement
possible_expedited_cannabis_expungement_request
mixed_cannabis_non_cannabis_case_admin_request
not_cannabis_use_regular_petition_route
needs_case_number_for_cannabis_aoc_request
Wilma script:
New Mexico has a special cannabis expungement process. If the case involved only cannabis or cannabis paraphernalia charges that are no longer crimes or would now be lesser offenses, it may be eligible for automatic or expedited automatic expungement. If the case also had non-cannabis charges, we may need to separate the eligible cannabis charges from the rest of the case.

sourceSections.title: Cannabis sentence dismissal / incarcerated-person pathway
sourceSections.text:
For people currently incarcerated for a cannabis offense that is no longer a crime or would have been a lesser offense under the Cannabis Regulation Act, correctional facilities, jails, or juvenile correctional facilities were required to notify the court that the case may be reopened for dismissal of sentence or expungement. The court must reopen and dismiss the sentence if it is legally invalid. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-9/")
A person who completed a sentence for a cannabis conviction that would no longer be an offense or would be a lesser offense is entitled to have the conviction dismissed and expunged because the prior conviction is legally invalid or redesignated as a penalty assessment citation. A person currently or formerly incarcerated for a cannabis offense that is no longer a crime may petition at any time to modify the sentence or vacate the conviction, and no fee or cost may be imposed for sentence review under this section. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-9/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_cannabis_sentence_dismissal
possible_cannabis_vacatur
possible_cannabis_redesignation
incarcerated_or_formerly_incarcerated_cannabis_handoff
no_fee_cannabis_sentence_review

sourceSections.title: DNA sample/profile expungement
sourceSections.text:
DNA sample/profile expungement is separate from the ordinary court-record expungement. A person may request expungement of their DNA sample and DNA records from New Mexicos DNA identification system if the conviction that led to the sample was reversed, or if the arrest that led to the sample resulted in dismissal, nolle prosequi, successful pre-prosecution diversion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or did not result in a felony charge within one year. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")
The person must provide a written request and certified documentation, such as a certified copy of the dismissal/nolle prosequi/diversion/conditional discharge/misdemeanor conviction/acquittal, or a sworn affidavit that no felony charges were filed within one year. If DNA records are expunged from the state DNA identification system, the administrative center must ensure expungement from CODIS. DNA expungement is not available if the person has a prior felony conviction or pending felony charge for which DNA collection is authorized. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")
Wilma status labels:
possible_dna_expungement
dna_conviction_reversed
dna_arrest_resolved_without_felony
dna_no_felony_charge_within_one_year
dna_block_prior_felony_or_pending_felony
needs_certified_disposition_or_affidavit

sourceSections.title: Effect of New Mexico expungement
sourceSections.text:
After an expungement order, proceedings are treated as if they never occurred, and officials and the person who received the order may respond to an inquiry that no record exists. However, arrest or conviction records must still be disclosed by the person and officials for applications or queries involving employment or association with a financial institution regulated by FINRA or the Securities and Exchange Commission. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-3a/section-29-3a-7/")
New Mexico court guidance also warns that courts, law enforcement agencies, and other criminal justice agencies will always have access to records even after expungement; records are not destroyed; and arrest or conviction records may still be available for use in future criminal proceedings.
Wilma plain-language script:
Expungement can help remove the record from public view and allow most public-facing responses to say no record exists. But it is not absolute. Criminal justice agencies may still access it, it may be used in future criminal proceedings, and financial-industry applications may still require disclosure.

sourceSections.title: Intake checklist for Wilma
sourceSections.text:
Identity
Full legal name.
Former names, aliases, nicknames.
Date of birth.
Current mailing address.
Address at time of incident, if known.
Phone and email.
Last four digits of SSN, especially for cannabis AOC request.
Picture ID.
Drivers license number if traffic/DWI/Motor Vehicle Code issue.
Case information
County.
Judicial district.
Court name: district, magistrate, metropolitan, municipal.
Case number.
Arrest date.
Arresting law enforcement agency.
Law enforcement agency case number.
Arrest number, if available.
District attorney / prosecuting agency.
Original charge.
Final charge.
Disposition.
Final disposition date.
Whether there was a conviction.
Whether the case involved cannabis, cannabis paraphernalia, or non-cannabis charges.
Whether case involved DWI/DUI.
Whether case involved child victim, sex offense, great bodily harm, death, embezzlement, or household-member offense.
Conviction-specific
Conviction level: municipal ordinance, misdemeanor, fourth-degree felony, third-degree felony, second-degree felony, first-degree felony.
Sentence date.
Sentence completion date.
Probation/parole completion date.
Whether fines/fees owed to the state were paid.
Whether victim restitution was ordered and completed.
Any criminal conviction during the waiting period.
Any pending charge or proceeding anywhere.
Documents
DPS record of arrest and prosecutions.
Court docket / Case Lookup printout.
Judgment and sentence.
Final disposition.
Proof of sentence completion.
Proof of probation/parole discharge.
Proof of fines/fees payment.
Proof of restitution completion.
Cannabis case documentation, if available.
Identity-theft proof, if applicable.
Certified disposition or affidavit for DNA expungement, if applicable.

sourceSections.title: Packet logic for RCAP/Wilma
sourceSections.text:
Identity theft
Generate/check:
1 Form 4-951 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Identity Theft
2 Supporting identity-theft documentation.
3 Records showing the wrongly identified arrest/case.
4 Proposed order.
5 Notice/hearing paperwork as required locally.
Release without conviction
Generate/check:
1 Form 4-952 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Upon Release Without Conviction
2 DPS record of arrest and prosecutions dated within 90 days.
3 Final disposition.
4 Certificate/service to district attorney and DPS.
5 Proposed order.
6 Notice of hearing or local hearing documents.
7 Indigency request, if needed.
Conviction
Generate/check:
1 Form 4-953 - Petition to Expunge Arrest Records and Public Records; Upon Conviction
2 DPS record / criminal history.
3 Judgment and sentence.
4 Proof of sentence completion.
5 Proof fines/fees paid.
6 Proof restitution completed.
7 Service to district attorney, DPS, and arresting agency.
8 Statement explaining why justice will be served.
9 Proposed order.
10 Indigency request, if needed.
Cannabis
Use the New Mexico Courts cannabis expungement application/AOC process first when the case is cannabis-only or cannabis-paraphernalia-only. If the case includes non-cannabis charges, route to the AOC cannabis request for eligible cannabis charges and separately screen non-cannabis charges under the regular court-petition process. ("https://nmcourts.gov/court-administration/office-of-general-counsel/expungement/")
DNA
Generate/check:
1 Written request for DNA sample/profile expungement.
2 Certified copy of dismissal, nolle prosequi, diversion completion, conditional discharge, misdemeanor conviction, acquittal, or reversal order.
3 Sworn affidavit if no felony charge was filed within one year.
4 Screen for prior felony conviction or pending felony charge, which can block DNA expungement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/new-mexico/chapter-29/article-16/section-29-16-10/")
